Great. All right. I'll hand that time over to you now. Thank you, Linda. Awesome. Well yeah, thanks for having me speak today. Um, so my name's Dr. Linda Di. I'm a GP and the founder of Meoc, which is a private menopause clinic here in New Zealand. Every day I hear from women navigating perimenopause, menopause, and menopause.

Often after years of feeling unheard, dismissed or told their symptoms were simply something they had to get on with. Menopause though is a huge and inevitable phase that every woman will go through. For some of those women, the symptoms can impact her job, her marriage, her physical and mental health.

Yet menopause has long been under recognized and poorly supported. As you know, this hearing came about because of the make menopause Matter petition from Bro Smith. There were three main requests in that petition, and given that I'm a doctor, it probably makes sense for me to focus on first request where she asked for mandatory menopause training for all GPS and medical students.

I agree that medical school curriculums and the GP training program need to have more menopause education embedded within them. I didn't receive enough training about menopause at medical school in the uk nor when I completed GP training program here in New Zealand. But when it comes to our currently prescribing gps, I'm not sure mandatory training is the way to go.

As someone who's worked in general practice myself, I know the practical reality, our gps are already stretched beyond capacity and if we mandate additional training, it's just gonna add further pressure to a system that's already busting at the seams and struggling. So what can we do instead? Well, I've got two suggestions.

The first involves taking a similar approach to something we already do for another massive phase. Many women go through pregnancy. When I was a working gp, we were able to present our newly pregnant ladies with a pregnancy pack, like a goodie bag that contained quality information and practical resources to help women understand how to have a happy, healthy pregnancy.

And I think menopause deserves the same approach. This menopause pack could contain clear information about symptoms, treatment options, lifestyles, changes, and pathways to further support it would normalize the menopause experience, empower women with the right knowledge and reduce the burden on GPS to deliver all this information in a limited consultation.

The second way I think we could improve menopause care for women in New Zealand is to consider funding specialist menopause clinics. This has already happened in the UK and in certain parts of Australia too. Funded access to specialist clinics would allow women to get expert care without personal cost, with no additional work placed onto general practice.

And without waiting several years for medical school, GP training changes to filter through into the current workforce. So this combination of a national menopause information pack and funded menopause clinics provides an approach to support women now offering quality information at scale, um, and specialist individual care when it's needed.

Um, so that's my spiel over, so I'm happy to take questions, um, if anyone's got any. Thank you. Uh, Linda, Jenny. Oh, thank you very much. Uh, Linda, I really appreciate, um, the time that you've spent today with us. Um, I'm interested to know what would be in the menopause pack.

Mm-hmm. Is this something you've already put together or is this No Recommendations on what should be in it? But in the menopause pack we've gotta have information about, you know, what the symptoms look like, what are the different treatment options that have evidence behind them.

And I don't just mean HRT, but that would clearly have a big part in that. 'cause a lot of women don't understand HRT, A lot of doctors don't understand HRT, but also non-hormonal therapies and psychological stuff, lifestyle stuff. You could have some pelvic floor exercises in there. You could have a big section on what your vagina's gonna do.

'cause women don't understand that side of things. You could have samples of things in there. I would love to give every woman over 40 a sample of a vest in cream, which is vaginal estrogen. I dunno if we could get that in the pack. You know, there's, there's lots of avenues to explore. You could have a, a thing about how to talk to your husband about menopause. Um, um, workplace support ideas.

Um, so it's still a varied sort of potluck. If a woman pays to see a gynecologist or an endocrinologist, she doesn't necessarily get all the right up to date information. But that in itself is a little bit misleading because it doesn't mean they've done any training, reading, gone to any lectures, gone to any conferences. And the issue here that we do actually have is that when we don't include young onset dementia, it becomes invisible in the system and it is invisible in the system.
And that means that we have to make allowances and often we default to aged care settings. So what we need is a recognition of that.

So young onset dementia, when it's not explicitly mentioned, creates a few problems 'cause we're at a different life stage when we have young onset dementia, and we need that age appropriate reaction or policies in place for that. So for example, people with young onset dementia are under 65 years old when they first experienced dementia.

And that means that they, um, have employment and income matching that, and they may have dependent children and housing expenses to go with that as well, and different physical economic and social needs as well. it can actually produce harm. It's not a neutral effect. And also downstream, it can actually increase costs both for, um, people that are, are giving the support and the services such as government and also for the person with young onset dementia. And they're far.

And I've in, in the actual oral briefing, I'll combine the next stage from the weeks after through to the first 12 months. And then it steps through the stages as dementia progresses to eventually end of life stage. Towards the bottom of that figure, you'll see the compounding effects.

I've used the, um, idea of getting an early diagnosis. If we don't get an early diagnosis, what will happen is that we delay the diagnosis. Dementia becomes more pro progressive. It means that that has effect and that compounds all the way through to, um, the latest stages, increased costs, increased crisis, more likely crisis, and also more likely carer burnout.

So if we can actually look at some of those things together, that would be good. So there's three things I think as a package, a early starting package that we could actually do as a country and as committee could recommend. The first is timely diagnosis is a system quality issue.

New Zealand research shows that it takes 3.6 years on average to diagnose young onset dementia from first signs or symptoms of that dementia. That's around two years longer than for older people with dementia that is over 60 fives or typically in the seventies and eighties.

What that means is that we've got that flow on effect happening. I think there are already some things we're working on that I know the health, health community, the medical community is actually working on. They're updating the clinical pathways for young onset dementia.

They have been for quite some time. And really what we need to do though now, currently at regional, each region has their own one. We need a national clinical pathway. Not only that, we also need GP education, and that's really up to Health New Zealand.

So if we get those two things really going, that actually moves the dial and it actually doesn't cost or the expense isn't that high, we often look at young onset dementia and go, Ooh, that might be a bit hard to solve. But I actually think that's one thing we could do. The second is, um, in the first weeks to 12 months after diagnosis, we need a minimum national offer.

Now we all know that every region and even district has different levels of capability, but if we have that minimum offer, we can also, um, at least have some minimum standards in place that different regions of, um, health New Zealand can actually work on.

And we also need social support. So my idea is to have, um, a named navigator assigned to that person, newly diagnosed as they do at Dementia Canterbury and a few other places, and they stay with them right through as much as possible for that time.

It's really in the first weeks to 12 months that it's quite intensive as the far now and the person get used to this. And we use that term dementia rehabilitation for that. The third thing that I ask is you've actually put in, uh, your report, um, as example Rotary Care Village.

So these are three things I think you could actually do as a committee. Then you need two tests in place. One is the live reality.
